slides.title: Forces Vocabulary
slides.content:
A force is a push or a pull that makes things move. Let's learn the key words:

👉 push — when you move something away from you
👈 pull — when you move something towards you
🏃 movement — when something changes position
🌍 ground — the surface of the earth we walk on
slides.tip: Push = empujar. Pull = tirar. Forces make things start, stop, or change direction!

slides.title: More Forces Words
slides.content:
More vocabulary about forces and movement:

🏐 throw — to send something through the air with your hand
🚀 speed — how fast something moves
🛑 brakes — the parts of a bike or car that make it stop

Examples:
I throw the ball to my friend.
The car has great speed.
Use the brakes to stop your bike!

slides.title: Things We Push and Pull
slides.content:
We use forces every day with these objects:

🖱️ computer mouse — you push it across the desk
📱 tablet — you push the screen with your finger
🧳 suitcase — you pull it behind you

Everything that moves needs a force!
slides.tip: Look around you! Can you find things you push and things you pull?

slides.title: Forces in the Playground
slides.html:
At break time, the children use forces everywhere!

Leo pushes the swing to make it go forward. Sara pulls the rope in a tug-of-war game. Max throws a ball high into the air.

The ball goes up, slows down, and then falls back to the ground. That's because gravity pulls everything down!

When the bell rings, everyone runs back inside. They use the brakes on their feet to stop at the door!

slides.title: Comparative Adjectives
slides.content:
Comparative adjectives help us compare two things:

For short adjectives (1 syllable), add -er:

🚀 fast → faster
🐢 slow → slower
🏋️ heavy → heavier
🪶 light → lighter

Example: A car is faster than a bike.
slides.tip: Short adjective + ER + than. Remember: if the adjective ends in -y, change it to -ier!

slides.title: More Comparatives
slides.content:
Some more comparative adjectives:

😊 easy → easier
😓 hard → harder

We always use than after the comparative:

Pushing a box is easier than pulling it.
Stopping a truck is harder than stopping a bike.
slides.tip: Comparative = we compare TWO things. Always use THAN between them!

slides.title: Compare the Objects
slides.problem:
Complete the sentences with the comparative form:

1. A feather is _____ (light) than a book.
2. A train is _____ (fast) than a car.
3. An elephant is _____ (heavy) than a cat.
4. Running is _____ (hard) than walking.
steps: 3. heavy --> change y to i + er = heavier

slides.title: The Big Race
slides.html:
Tom and Lily have a race on their bikes. Tom's bike is faster than Lily's bike, but Lily's bike is lighter than Tom's.

Going downhill, Tom is faster because his bike is heavier. But going uphill, Lily is faster because her bike is lighter!

At the end, they both use their brakes to stop. Stopping a heavier bike is harder than stopping a lighter one. Tom needs to push his brakes harder!

slides.title: Push or Pull?
slides.problem:
Decide if each action is a push or a pull:

1. Opening a drawer.
2. Kicking a football.
3. Dragging a suitcase.
4. Pressing a button on a tablet.
steps: 1. You move the drawer towards you --> pull
2. You move the ball away from you with your foot --> push
3. You move the suitcase towards you --> pull
4. You press the screen away from you --> push

slides.title: Forces Change Movement
slides.content:
Forces can change how things move:

▶️ A push or pull can make something start moving
⏹️ A force can make something stop (like brakes)
⏫ A force can make something go faster (more speed)
⏬ A force can make something go slower
↩️ A force can change direction
slides.tip: Without forces, nothing would ever move or stop!

slides.title: Let's Review!
points: 💪 Forces: push (away from you) and pull (towards you)
🏃 Vocabulary: movement, ground, throw, speed, brakes
🖥️ We push: computer mouse, tablet. We pull: suitcase
🚀 faster, slower, heavier, lighter, easier, harder
🔄 Forces can start, stop, speed up, slow down, or change direction
